title: How Much Protein Do You Need in a Day? An Indian Vegetarian Guide
subtitle: Most Indian plates are lower in protein than people assume, and the fix is simpler than buying a supplement.
meta_title: How Much Protein Do You Need in a Day? Indian Guide
meta_description: How much protein do you need in a day? Here is the ICMR based answer for Indians, plus simple dal, curd and soya swaps that actually work.
content:
Most healthy Indian adults need about 0.83 grams of protein per kilogram of body weight each day. That is the figure ICMR-NIN uses in its recommended dietary allowances, so a 60 kg adult needs close to 50 grams daily. The harder question is not how much protein do you need in a day, but whether your usual thali is quietly falling short of that number.

Why it matters in India

Indian diets are built around cereals. Rice, wheat and refined flour products make up the bulk of most plates, and pulses often appear as a thin dal poured over the top. Cereals do carry some protein, but how much you actually get depends on how much of the plate they occupy and how watery that dal is.

Vegetarian and vegan eating is common here, and that is not a problem by itself. Plant foods can cover your protein needs comfortably. The gap usually comes from portion size and repetition: the same small katori of dal, day after day, with very little paneer, curd, soya, egg or fish alongside it.

Older adults, pregnant and breastfeeding women, teenagers and people recovering from illness need more than the baseline. If you are in one of these groups, ask a doctor or a registered dietitian for a target that fits your situation rather than guessing.

How much protein do you need in a day if you exercise?

The 0.83 g/kg figure is set for a sedentary or lightly active adult. If you lift weights, run regularly or train for a sport, your requirement rises. Sports nutrition bodies generally place active adults between 1.2 and 2.0 grams per kilogram, with the higher end for people doing heavy strength training or cutting calories while trying to hold on to muscle.

For a 65 kg person who trains four times a week, that is roughly 80 to 110 grams a day instead of about 54. This is a big jump, and it explains why many people feel their training is not showing results even though they are working hard in the gym.

Which Indian foods actually give you protein?

A few familiar foods do most of the work.

  • Soya chunks and granules: the highest protein plant food in most Indian kitchens, and inexpensive. A small handful bulks up considerably once soaked.
  • Pulses and legumes: moong, masoor, toor, chana, rajma and lobia. A thicker dal gives you more protein per katori than a watery one.
  • Dairy: curd, paneer, milk and chaas. Paneer is one of the easiest ways to lift the protein in a vegetarian meal.
  • Eggs and fish: for those who eat them, both are efficient, affordable and quick to cook.
  • Nuts and seeds: peanuts, almonds, pumpkin seeds and til. Useful, though they bring plenty of fat and calories with them.
  • Whole grains: jowar, bajra, ragi and whole wheat add up steadily across the day even though no single serving is high.

Pairing a cereal with a pulse in the same meal improves the quality of the protein you absorb. Dal chawal, rajma chawal, idli with sambar, khichdi and roti with chana all follow this pattern. Indian home cooking got this right long before anyone wrote it down.

How do you build a high protein vegetarian day?

You do not need powders or imported foods. You need some protein at every meal instead of only at lunch.

Here is a sample day for a moderately active adult:

  • Breakfast: two moong dal chillas with a bowl of curd, or paneer bhurji with one roti.
  • Mid morning: a glass of milk or chaas and a handful of roasted chana.
  • Lunch: two rotis, a thick dal or rajma, a sabzi and a bowl of curd.
  • Evening: boiled eggs, sprouts chaat or roasted peanuts.
  • Dinner: soya chunk curry or paneer with rice or roti and a vegetable.

If your current day looks nothing like this, start with three swaps rather than a full overhaul:

  1. Make your dal thicker and serve yourself a bigger katori.
  2. Replace one biscuit or namkeen snack with curd, sprouts or roasted chana.
  3. Add a protein food to breakfast, which is the meal where most Indians get almost none.
A useful check: if you cannot name the protein source in a meal you just ate, that meal probably did not have one.

Myths worth dropping

Protein damages your kidneys. In healthy people, normal protein intake does not harm the kidneys. If you already have kidney disease, your protein target must be set by your doctor.

You need a supplement to hit your protein. Most people reach their target with regular food. A powder is a convenience for those with a high requirement and a tight schedule, not a necessity.

Vegetarians cannot get enough protein. They can, and many do. It takes planning and slightly larger portions, not a change of diet.

Extra protein turns into muscle on its own. Protein supports repair, but muscle comes from training. Without resistance work, extra grams are simply extra calories.

Frequently asked questions

How much protein do you need in a day to lose weight?

During weight loss, protein helps you stay full and protects muscle while you eat fewer calories. Many people do well nearer 1.2 to 1.6 grams per kilogram, paired with strength training. Speak to a dietitian before making a large change if you have a medical condition.

Can I get all my protein from dal alone?

It is possible in theory but hard in practice, because the quantity required is very large. Spreading intake across dal, curd, paneer, soya and whole grains is easier on your appetite and your digestion.

Is too much protein bad for you?

Very high intakes over long periods are unnecessary and can crowd out foods you also need, such as fibre rich vegetables and whole grains. Staying inside the ranges above suits almost everyone.

Does soaking or sprouting pulses change anything?

Soaking and sprouting reduce compounds that block mineral absorption and make pulses easier to digest. The protein content stays broadly the same, but you absorb nutrients better.

Should I eat protein right after a workout?

Total daily intake matters far more than exact timing. Spreading protein fairly evenly across your meals is a sensible approach for most people.

Getting enough protein from an Indian kitchen is mostly about portion sizes and a few repeated habits, not exotic ingredients. Count what you eat on a normal day, compare it with your weight based target, then fix the meal with the biggest gap first. For structured plans and tools built around Indian food, take a look at Dietary Guide.
